text.title: Index Optimization
text.summary: Composite index column ordering, covering indexes, partial indexes, and expression indexes.

rule.statement: In composite indexes, place equality-filter columns first, range-filter columns last.
rule.rationale: The B-tree can use prefix columns for equality and then traverse a range on the last column. Placing range columns first makes the index unusable for the equality filter.

example.code: -- Range first -- index used only for created_at range, not user_id equality: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_created_user ON orders(created_at, user_id);

rule.statement: Use INCLUDE columns to add non-filtered columns to an index to avoid heap lookups for read-heavy queries.
rule.rationale: A covering index satisfies the query entirely from index pages, eliminating the heap fetch (table lookup) for each matched row.

example.code: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_covering ON orders(user_id, status) INCLUDE (total_amount, created_at);

rule.statement: Use partial indexes with a WHERE clause for queries that always filter on a specific value.
rule.rationale: Partial indexes are smaller (fewer rows indexed) and faster to scan than full indexes. Useful for status columns where only one value (e.g. 'pending') is queried frequently.

example.code: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending ON orders(created_at) WHERE status = 'pending';

rule.statement: Create an expression index when queries apply a function to an indexed column; do not apply functions to indexed columns in WHERE clauses.
rule.rationale: WHERE LOWER(email) = ? bypasses the regular index on email. An expression index on LOWER(email) makes the query use the index.

example.code: CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_lower ON users(LOWER(email)); -- Query: WHERE LOWER(email) = 'test@example.com' -- uses the index
